coredata.dc:title: A green method for the preparation of carbon quantum dots from yellow lupin peel waste for spectrofluorometric determination of nirmatrelvir
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Alomar, Taghrid
AlMasoud, Najla
Mansour, Fotouh R.
coredata.dc:description: The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the urgent need for effective antiviral agents. One such promising drug is nirmatrelvir (NIRMA), which has demonstrated the ability to inhibit viral replication and reduce hospitalization and mortality rates. In the field of analytical chemistry, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from fruit peel waste have gained attention for their eco-friendly and cost-effective properties. In this study, CQDs were synthesized from the peel biomass waste of yellow lupin and utilized for the spectrofluorometric determination of NIRMA in pharmaceutical tablets. The proposed method offers simplicity and speed compared to existing methods. The synthesis process involved washing and grinding the peel waste, followed by heating in propylene glycol and purification using a dialysis membrane. The resulting CQD solution showed fluorescence at an emission wavelength of 411 nm upon excitation at 325 nm. The validation of the method, including linearity, range, limit of detection and quantification, accuracy, precision, and robustness, was conducted following ICH guidelines. The fluorescence intensities exhibited a linear relationship with increasing NIRMA concentration in the range of 15–150 µg/mL. The limit of detection found to be 4.0 μg/mL, with %relative standard deviations ≤ 1.46. This spectrofluorometric method represents a novel approach for the determination of NIRMA using CQDs derived from yellow lupin peel waste.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Natural
Carbon quantum dots
Nirmatrelvir
Microwave
Fluorescence
COIVID-19

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopic image (a), fourier transform infrared spectrum (b) and zeta potential (c) of the prepared NACQDs.

Overlaid spectra of the excitation (solid) and emission (dashed) of the prepared CQDs in the absence (Black) and the presence of NIRMA (Red). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Surface response charts of the incubation times, NACQD volumes, ionic strength and pH.

An overlay of different concentrations of NIRMA in NACQDs prepared from yellow lupin. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The green analytical procedure index (GAPI) of the proposed method (left) and the reference method (right). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Method assessment using AGREE (left) and BAGI metrics (Right).
The regression data of the developed spectrofluorimetric method for analysis of NIRMA.
Accuracy, intraday and interday precision of nirmatrelvir determination by the proposed method.
Robustness study of the proposed method.
Comparison between the developed method and the reference HPLC/UV method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of triplicate determinations for each concentration

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Evaluation of the environmental friendliness of the new method using the analytical Eco-scale.

head.ce:title: A green method for the preparation of carbon quantum dots from yellow lupin peel waste for spectrofluorometric determination of nirmatrelvir

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, 31111, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Elgeish Street, the medical campus of Tanta University, Tanta, 31111, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: the medical campus of Tanta University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the urgent need for effective antiviral agents. One such promising drug is nirmatrelvir (NIRMA), which has demonstrated the ability to inhibit viral replication and reduce hospitalization and mortality rates. In the field of analytical chemistry, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from fruit peel waste have gained attention for their eco-friendly and cost-effective properties. In this study, CQDs were synthesized from the peel biomass waste of yellow lupin and utilized for the spectrofluorometric determination of NIRMA in pharmaceutical tablets. The proposed method offers simplicity and speed compared to existing methods. The synthesis process involved washing and grinding the peel waste, followed by heating in propylene glycol and purification using a dialysis membrane. The resulting CQD solution showed fluorescence at an emission wavelength of 411 nm upon excitation at 325 nm. The validation of the method, including linearity, range, limit of detection and quantification, accuracy, precision, and robustness, was conducted following ICH guidelines. The fluorescence intensities exhibited a linear relationship with increasing NIRMA concentration in the range of 15–150 µg/mL. The limit of detection found to be 4.0 μg/mL, with %relative standard deviations ≤ 1.46. This spectrofluorometric method represents a novel approach for the determination of NIRMA using CQDs derived from yellow lupin peel waste.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Across the last few years, the world has been challenged with the pervasive transmission of the novel coronavirus (COVID-19). Public reports from the World Health Organization (WHO) have revealed that by December 2023, the number of individuals infected worldwide surpassed an alarming 770 million cases [1,2]. For these reasons, appreciable efforts have been exerted to develop new antiviral agents with evidence-based effects against COVID-19 [3,4]. These efforts led to the approval of a few drugs, among which nirmatrelvir (NIRMA) is highly promising protease inhibitor to impede protein processing and consequently hinder viral replication. Clinical trials demonstrated high potential in mitigating hospitalization and mortality rates among patients exhibiting moderate manifestation of COVID-19 [5,6].

Lately, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained increasing attention in various applications due to their distinct features like affordability, eco-friendly nature, low toxicity, simple preparation, and water solubility [7–9]. CQDs generally consist of carbon cores and surface groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine, and sulfahydryl groups. These versatile CQDs have found extensive use in bioimaging [10–12], water treatment [13–16] and drug analysis [17–19].

Preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from fruit peel waste is a promising approach towards development of green analytical methods. This approach converts this typically discarded organic waste into valuable CQDs through a simple and cost-effective process, which contributes to minimizing waste generation and utilizing renewable resources. The production of CQDs from fruit peel waste supports sustainability by reducing the reliance on non-renewable carbon sources. This in turn mitigates the carbon footprint associated with traditional synthesis methods.

Several peels of fruits and seeds have been employed for the preparation of NACQDs, including citrus [20], banana [21], lemon [22], kiwi [23], and orange [24]. In this work, we use the peel waste of yellow lupin for the first time as a source to prepare NACQDs. The aim of this study is to apply these NACQDs in the spectrofluorometric determination of NIRMA in pharmaceutical tablets. The proposed method is simpler and faster in comparison to the other reported methods for NIRMA determination. As far as we know, this is the first spectrofluorometric method for the determination of NIRMA.

NIRMA (99.8) was graciously provided by Global Napi Pharmaceuticals located in 6th of October City, Egypt. The chemicals, NaOH (99 %), KI (99 %), and Quinine sulfate, were procured from ISO-CHEM laboratory and used as received without any further treatment. H2SO4 and HCl were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). Distilled water was used for all the experiments. Paxlovid® tablets, manufactured by Pfizer, Inc. (Egypt), were purchased from the local market.

For the measurement of fluorescence spectra, an FP-6300 spectrofluorometer from JASCO (Tokyo, Japan) was employed. UV absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-1800 double beam spectrophotometer by Shimadzu (Kyoto, Japan). Characterization of the synthesized NACQDs involved the use of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) from Tokyo, Japan, and Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) from Utah, United States. To analyze the zeta potential, a ZetaPALS potential analyzer manufactured by Brookhaven (USA) was utilized. The synthesis of NACQDs involved the use of a Tornado microwave oven (model MOM-C36BBE-S, 1000 W) and a benchtop centrifuge from Hettich (Tuttlingen, Germany). The pH measurements were carried out using a pH-meter from HANNA (Woonsocket, Rhode Island, USA) with a double junction glass electrode calibrated by standard buffers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of standard and working solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The stock standard solutions for NIRMA were individually made at a concentration of 1.0 mg/mL in methanol and kept at 4 °C until required. Portions of this stock solutions were then transferred to 25 mL volumetric flasks and diluted with deionized water to reach final concentrations in the range 15 to 150 μg/mL.

A few grams of the peel waste of annual yellow-lupin (Lupinus luteus) were washed with distilled water, dried in domestic oven at 90 °C for 2 h and then well ground using mortar and pestle. An amount of 1.0 g of the ground peel was weighed, transferred to a crucible filled with 10 mL of propylene glycol. Then the solution was heated in the microwave oven at 900 W for 60 s until the formation of a yellowish colored solution. Then, a dialyzing membrane possessing a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) value of 10 kDa was employed to achieve purification and homogenization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The primary objective of this process was to eliminate residual precursors as well as byproducts consisting of large molecules. Through the utilization of the dialysis bag, the residues were effectively retained within, enabling the collection of a clear solution of CQDs beyond its confines. 1 mL of this clear solution was withdrawn, transferred to a 10 mL volumetric flask, and completed to the mark with distilled water. Alternatively, the solution tube can be centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 15 min to eliminate residues and ensure homogeneity. In this case, 50 μL should be takes of the CQDs before being completed to the mark with double distilled water. The fluorescence intensities were measured at an emission wavelength of 411 nm after excitation at 325 nm. Figure S1 summarizes the preparation procedure of of NACQDs from yellow lupin.

The measurement of quantum yield (Qu) involved the utilization of quinine sulfate in a 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 solution as a reference, where the value of Qst was determined to be 0.54. The calculation of Qu was performed using the equation:
Q u
=
Q
st
Fu
Fst
Ast
Au
η u
η s t
2
In the context of this equation, F denotes the fluorescence intensity following excitation at 330 nm, A represents the absorbance values at the same wavelength, and η corresponds to the refractive index of the solvent. It should be noted that the refractive index for both solvents is 1.33

A stock containing NIRMA was prepared by dissolving 0.01 g of NIRMA in 10 mL of distilled water, resulting in a concentration of 1.0 mg/mL. Subsequently, varying volumes of the NIRMA solution (ranging from 150 to 1500 µL) were added to 50 µL of NACQDs in a 10 mL flask to achieve a final NIRMA concentration between 15 and 150 µg/mL. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 7 using 0.1 mol/L sodium hydroxide. The fluorescence intensities were promptly measured at 411 nm following excitation at 325 nm. A blank solution was prepared in a 10 mL flask by adding 50 µL of NACQDs and then filling the flask up to volume with distilled water. A calibration curve was established by correlating the concentration of NIRMA with the difference in fluorescence intensity (ΔF = F − F0), where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of NACQDs in the presence and absence of NIRMA, respectively.

The procedures for assay of tablet were as follows, in 100 mL volumetric flask, an amount equivalent to one tablet (150 mg) was sonicated in 80 mL methanol for 40 mins to ensure complete liberation of the nirmatrelvir. After the system cooling at room temperature, the volume was completed to 100 with methanol. The supernatant was filtered by syringe filter (0.45 µm), then 200, 600 and 800 µL was transferred to 10 mL volumetric flask, and the volume was completed by deionized water till 10 mL to obtain 30, 90 and 120 µg/mL of NIRMA, respectively. The concentration was assayed 6 times on HPLC and the developed spectrofluorimetric method.

In this study, the microwave-assisted method was successfully employed for the synthesis of NACQDs with highly fluorescent properties. The reaction time was remarkably shortened to just one minute, allowing for rapid production. Notably, the synthesis was accomplished using cost-effective and easily obtainable starting materials, as shown in Figure S1 .

The quantum yield of the NACQDs was determined to be 2.31 %, indicating their ability to efficiently emit fluorescence. Upon exposure to UV light, the NACQDs demonstrated a distinct blue fluorescence, which remained consistent over a period of 20 min. To elucidate their morphological characteristics, as well as the distribution of particle sizes, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was conducted at a magnification power of 20 nm. The obtained TEM images revealed that the NACQDs exhibited well-dispersed spherical shapes, with minimal aggregation observed between particles and an average particle size of 2.47 nm.

For a thorough understanding of the chemical structure properties of the NACQDs, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was employed over the range 500–4000 cm−1. The resulting IR spectrum provided valuable information regarding the stretching frequencies associated with various functional groups. Notably, stretching vibrations corresponding to O–H (3500–3100 cm−1), C–H (2997 cm−1), C = O (1664 cm−1), C = C (1462 cm−1), C-O (1138 cm−1), and C–C (1050 cm−1) were identified, as displayed in Fig. 1
b.

To further characterize the NACQDs, elemental analysis employing a CHNS elemental analyzer was performed. The results revealed that the composition of NACQDs comprised approximately 76.46 % carbon (C) and 23.54 % oxygen (O). These findings suggest the presence of a carbon core and surface hydroxyl and carboxyl groups within the NACQDs, which exhibits a high degree of water solubility. The zeta potential revealed negative charge which is congruent with the presence of carboxyl groups and guarantees good dispersibility of the prepared NACQDs. The NACQDs' fluorescence spectrum was examined across various excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 390 nm. The emission intensity peaked at 411 nm when stimulated at 325 nm. The main reason for this excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior is likely the presence of diverse surface groups on the NACQDs [25]. Following the addition of NIRMA, a notable increase in the fluorescence intensity of NACQDs was observed, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . This augmentation in fluorescence is attributed to the electrostatic interaction and electron transfer between the hydroxyl groups on the NACQDs' surface and the polar functional groups present in NIRMA, such as fluoride, carbonyl, and nitrile groups ( Figure S2
) [26].

The experimental conditions were initially optimized using factorial design utilizing Design Expert® Stat Ease software to get the highest fluorescence enhancement in the presence of NIRMA. The factors investigated were NACQDs volume (in μL), incubation duration between NIRMA and the dots (in min), the ionic strength (in g%) and the diluent pH. The programme assisted in identifying the important experimental variables and forecasting the fluorescence intensity and the precision. The software suggested 25 different runs with different incubation times (1–10 min), NACQD volumes (25–150 μL), ionic strength (0.1–1 g%) and pH. Response surfaces were generated for the emission intensity and the %RSD. The objective of the model was to maximize the response, while keeping the %RSD equal or below 2 %, as per the ICH Q2 (R2) guidelines. As shown in Fig. 3 , a significant increase in response was observed with the decrease in NACQD volumes, while minor increases were observed with high pH values, short incubation times and high ionic strengths. On the other hand, %RSD > 2 were observed with small volumes of NACQDs which could be due to the high risk of error with small volumes of the fluorescent probe. To evaluate the optimization model, the analysis of variance (ANOVA) of the model and the studied experimental conditions were calculated. As shown in Table S1 , the optimum conditions included an incubation time of 1 min, an NACQDs volume of 60 µL, an ionic strength of 1 g% and a diluent pH of 9.

The method was developed by plotting the response against different concentrations of NIRMA in the range 15 to 150 μg/mL. A proportional increase in emission was observed with the increase in NIRMA concentration, as shown in Fig. 4 .

The validity of the method was confirmed in accordance with the ICH guidelines [27] across various parameters including linearity, dynamic range, detection and quantification limits, precision, accuracy, and robustness.

Both the linearity and range were studied for the developed method. As the concentration of NIRMA increased within the 15–150 µg/mL range, there was a proportional linear increase in fluorescence intensities. Detailed regression parameters can be found in Table 1 .

Using the equations that involve the standard deviation of the intercept (σ) and the slope of the calibration curve (s), the values for the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) were determined to be 6.02 µg/mL and 18.79 µg/mL, respectively.

The accuracy of the analytical method reflects how closely the results align with the true value. To assess the accuracy of the proposed method, three varying concentrations of NIRMA were analyzed in triplicate within the linear range. Accuracy was gauged by calculating the % recovery, which, as per Table 2 , fell between 98–102 %—within the acceptable threshold.

Meanwhile, the precision of the proposed method was evaluated through intraday and interday precision measurements. Intraday precision was determined by running three replicates of three distinct NIRMA concentrations within the linear range on the same day. This process was repeated over three successive days to assess interday precision. The outcomes are detailed in Table 2, showcasing %RSD values below 2 %, indicating the method's precision.

To assess the method's robustness, we examined the impact of slight intentional variations in the parameters of the fluorescence response. The parameters under scrutiny included pH (9 ± 0.2), incubation time (varied in 0.5-minute intervals), emission wavelength (425 ± 2.0 nm), and excitation wavelength (311 ± 2.0 nm). These marginal alterations were observed to have negligible influence on the method's performance, underscoring its robust nature as indicated in Table 3 .

The developed method was successfully applied for the determination of NIRMA in pharmaceutical tablets. The %recoveries were found to be within the acceptable range (95–105 %) with %RSD less than 2 %. The results were compared with reported HPLC method [28] and statistically compared using both Student t-test and F-tests. No significant statistical differences were observed, which proves that the method was comparably accurate and precise to the reported method (Table 4 ). However, the developed spectrofluorimetric method is faster, easier to perform and does not require the sophisticated instruments or toxic reagents and solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green assessment of the developed method
ce:section.ce:para: The green assessment of the proposed method was evaluated using three different assessment tools: the analytical eco scale [29], and the green analytical procedure index (GAPI) [30] and Analytical Greenness metric approach (AGREE) [31]. The analytical eco-scale generates a score by deducting penalty points from a base value of 100, where penalties are assigned based on solvent and reagent usage, energy consumption, occupational hazards, and waste generation. The proposed method scored 88 on the eco-scale, indicating its significant green attributes as illustrated in Table 5 . The GAPI tool utilizes a color-coded pictogram (green, yellow, and red) to categorize the environmental impact of each step in the analytical process. Fig. 5 displays the GAPI pictogram for the proposed method, and the reference method. The AGREE tool [31] and the Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI)metrics to further assess the greenness of the developed method and to evaluate the he practicability of the procedure [32]. The developed method employs water as a solvent during NACQD preparation which reduces the environmental impact. The total Analytical Eco-scale is 88, with a AGREE score of 75, and a BAGI score of 70.The AGREE score confirms the green nature of the developed method, while the BAGI score indicate that the method adequately practical for routine analysis of NIRMA.Fig. 6. .

The spectrofluorimetric determination of NIRMA can be challenging due to the lack of a natural fluorophore in the molecule. In the past, many techniques for analyzing NIRMA have relied on chromatographic separation methods using UV or mass detection within the HPLC setup. However, these HPLC-based methods have their drawbacks. They are sophisticated, time-consuming, not eco-friendly, and can be expensive [33–35]. In contrast, our newly developed method offers distinct advantages over traditional HPLC-based methods. This new method is simpler, more time-efficient, environmentally friendly, and does not require specialized equipment. One of the key advantages of the developed method is the simplicity of the synthesis of CQDs and the associated analytical procedures. The simplicity of CQDs synthesis and the procedures involved make this new method a preferable and more convenient choice compared to previous HPLC-based methods. By using CQDs as a fluorophore substitute, the method eliminates the need for complex chromatographic separations and the associated drawbacks of traditional HPLC techniques. This innovation not only simplifies the analytical process but also improves the overall efficiency of NIRMA determination. Generally, the newly developed method is a promising alternative for spectrofluorometric determination of NIRMA, offering advantages in terms of simplicity, time efficiency, eco-friendliness, and cost-effectiveness. It represents a step towards more sustainable and accessible analytical techniques in the field of chemical analysis.

In this study, NACQDs were successfully synthesized using a novel microwave-assisted method that utilized yellow lupin peels as a starting material. Remarkably, the synthesis process only took 60 s, showcasing the efficiency of the approach. The resulting NACQDs demonstrated a strong blue emission peak at 425 nm when excited at 311 nm, with a quantum yield of 2.31 %. The fluorescence intensities of the NACQDs exhibited a linear enhancement upon the addition of NIRMA in a concentration range of 15–150 µg/mL. This enhancement was attributed to the formation of a complex between the CQDS and NIRMA, indicating the potential of NACQDs as a fluorescent probe for the quantitative determination of NIRMA in pharmaceutical dosage forms. During the study, all factors influencing fluorescence stability and intensity were meticulously optimized to ensure reliable and accurate results. The developed method was successfully applied to determine NIRMA content in pharmaceutical tablets, yielding satisfactory results with percentage recoveries falling within the range of 95–105 %. The findings suggest that the proposed method is not only simple and fast but also selective and practical for real-world applications. The ability to use NACQDs synthesized from yellow lupin peels as a fluorescent probe for NIRMA determination in pharmaceutical formulations represents a significant advancement in analytical chemistry. The study showcases the potential of innovative approaches in material synthesis and analytical techniques for improving the efficiency and accuracy of pharmaceutical analysis, with the potential to be applied for the determination of NIRMA in environmental samples and biological fluids.
This research was funded by the Deanship of Scientific Research at Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Riyadh, Saudi Arabia (Researchers Supporting Project number PNURSP2024R47).

Taghrid Alomar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Najla AlMasoud: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Fotouh R. Mansour: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
